---
title: "Assemble an infectious disease model"
description: "Build a joint model from a latent process, infection mechanism, and observation layers — all through a single `as_turing_model` interface."
toc: true
toc-location: left
---

:::::: {.side-space}

::: {.section-start-space}

## The assembled way of thinking

An epidemiological model has three roles: a **latent** process (what drives
transmission over time), an **infection** process (how the latent process
generates infections), and an **observation** process (how infections become
the data you see). Each role is an interchangeable part; all parts speak the
same [`as_turing_model`](https://composableturingidmodels.epiaware.org/dev/)
interface, so they nest and combine freely.

The code below builds a complete model: an AR(1) process on the log
reproduction number feeds a renewal equation, and the resulting infections pass
through a reporting delay, ascertainment, and negative-binomial noise — all
in one self-contained `IDModel` assembly.

:::

::: {.code-example-column .section-start-space .section-end-space}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

:::

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>renewal_model.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposableTuringIDModels, Distributions, Turing

# Renewal infections with inline latent process
data = IDData(gen_distribution = Gamma(1.4, 1 / 0.38))

model = IDModel(
    Renewal(data;
        rt = AR(; ϵ_t = HierarchicalNormal(
            std_prior = HalfNormal(0.1))),
        initialisation_prior = Normal(log(1.0), 1.0)),

    # Multiple observation layers:
    #   delay → ascertainment → negative-binomial noise
    LatentDelay(
        Ascertainment(
            NegativeBinomialError(),
            ascertainment_prior = Beta(10, 2)),
        truncated(Normal(5.0, 2.0), 0.0, Inf)))

# Fit to case data with Turing's NUTS
chain = sample(as_turing_model(model, cases, length(cases)),
               NUTS(), MCMCThreads(), 1_000, 2)
```

:::
:::

## Why assemble a model?

- **Swap a part to change an assumption.** Replace `NegativeBinomialError()`
  with `PoissonError()` to test overdispersion; swap the renewal equation for
  `DirectInfections` to remove the generation-interval assumption. One-line
  changes, no model rewrites.
- **Transparent structure.** `IDModel` prints its nested parts, so the model
  architecture reads directly from the code and the output.
- **Simulate and infer from the same object.** Pass `missing` to
  `as_turing_model` to draw prior predictive simulations; pass real data to
  run NUTS inference.
- **Multiple observation layers.** Reporting delays, ascertainment,
  right-truncation, and day-of-week effects are composable modifiers that wrap
  the core observation model. Layers are added or removed independently.
- **Full Turing toolchain.** Every assembly becomes a
  [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org) model, so NUTS, Pathfinder, prior
  simulation, and the entire Turing ecosystem work unchanged.

## Where next

- Read the [ComposableTuringIDModels.jl overview](https://composableturingidmodels.epiaware.org/dev/overview/)
  for the design and the full part catalogue.
- See the [quickstart tutorial](https://composableturingidmodels.epiaware.org/dev/)
  for a walkthrough from installation to inference.
- Interested in the delay side of the model? See the
  [composed distributions approach](composed-distributions.qmd).

::::::
